title: AudioContext is never closed and unlock listeners never removed; reset() referenced in docs does not exist
evidence: unlockOnGesture() adds three window listeners (pointerdown/keydown/touchstart) that are never removed; AudioEngine has no close()/reset() despite the napalmStart() doc saying 'Must call napalmStop() (or reset())'. main.ts calls renderer.reset() on teardown but there is no audio.reset(). The AudioContext (a scarce browser resource, typically capped ~6/page) is created once and never ctx.close()'d.
impact: AudioEngine is a page singleton created once in bootstrap(), so this is not a per-game leak — the unlock listeners and single AudioContext persist for the whole session by design. The dangling reference to a non-existent reset() in the doc-comment is a correctness/maintenance trap: a future caller following the comment would call a method that doesn't exist (compile error) or assume napalm nodes get torn down on a reset that was never implemented, leaking the sustained napalmSrc/napalmGain nodes if napalm is alight at game teardown (no one calls napalmStop on quit mid-burn).
recommendation: Add a reset()/dispose() that calls napalmStop() and removes the unlock listeners (store handler refs); call it from main.ts teardown(). Or remove the stale 'or reset()' clause from the comment. If napalm can be burning at teardown, ensure napalmStop() runs so the looping source node is released.
acceptance_criteria: No doc references a method that does not exist
Sustained napalm nodes are torn down when a game ends mid-burn

title: rate-limit RPC fails OPEN on any DB error, removing the only abuse backstop for unauthenticated submit_action
evidence: enforceRateLimit() returns true (allowed) whenever supabase.rpc('bump_rate_limit') returns an error: `if (error) { console.error(...); return true /* fail open */ }`. All 10 Edge Functions are verify_jwt=false public endpoints (per migration 005 header).
impact: Intentional fail-open keeps the game playable during a limiter hiccup, but the blast radius is that a persistent rate_limits-table problem (e.g. the table missing, a permission regression, or sustained DB pressure) silently disables ALL per-IP limiting across every function at once — exactly when the DB is already stressed. The fail-open is correct for liveness; the risk is it is unobservable beyond a console.error (no metric/alert), so a degraded limiter can run indefinitely unnoticed.
impact_note: reliability lens only: this is a degraded-resilience observation, not the appsec authz review.
recommendation: Keep fail-open, but emit a distinguishable structured log/metric on the fail-open path so a chronically-erroring limiter is detectable, and consider a circuit-breaker that escalates after N consecutive RPC errors.
acceptance_criteria: Fail-open events are distinguishable from normal allow in logs/metrics

title: Referee trusts client-reported nextActiveIndex/roundOver with no engine-side validation; a wrong cursor can silently desync lockstep
evidence: The referee runs NO physics. It advances room.active_player_index using the client-reported nextActiveIndex (nextCursor) and relaxes/repoints the turn gate using the client-reported roundOver flag. authorizeAction()'s only turn gate is `activePlayer.id !== actingId` against that cursor. computeNextSeat() derives the index by cloning the LOCAL engine and ticking to completion — so its correctness depends entirely on the submitting client's engine being in the canonical state.
impact: If the submitting client's engine has diverged (any prior determinism bug, a partial replay, or a tampered client), it reports a wrong nextActiveIndex; the referee stores it verbatim, so the cursor that gates EVERY subsequent turn now points at the wrong seat. Honest clients then reject the wrongly-gated action ('Not your turn') and the game stalls, or accept an out-of-rotation action and the action log itself becomes the canonical-but-wrong history every client faithfully replays. The seq-unique constraint guards seq collisions but NOT a semantically-wrong cursor. This is the structural single-point-of-failure behind the P0-2/P0-3 class of bugs the code already patched piecemeal.
recommendation: Have the referee validate the reported next seat against a cheap server-side invariant where possible (e.g. it must be a member index, and for non-roundOver it must differ from the acting seat — nextCursor already does the latter; extend to reject a reported seat that is not alive per a server-tracked elimination set, or periodically checkpoint a hash of each client's engine state to detect divergence early). At minimum, log/alert when a submit is rejected as 'Not your turn' repeatedly for one room (a desync signature).
acceptance_criteria: A divergence between a client-reported cursor and the canonical rotation is detectable server-side or via a state-hash checkpoint
Repeated 'Not your turn' rejections for a room surface as a desync signal

title: submit_action seq-conflict retry uses Math.random() jitter — fine for network, but note the only non-deterministic call in the submit path
evidence: const delay = Math.min(SEQ_BACKOFF_MS * 2 ** attempt, 240) + Math.floor(Math.random() * 25). This is in the NETWORK retry scheduler, not the shared engine, so it does NOT affect lockstep determinism (the engine never sees it). Verified the engine (shared/) and AI use only seeded createRng; no Math.random/Date in shared/src/engine.
impact: No determinism impact (correctly outside shared/). Documented only to record that the engine determinism invariant holds: the sole Math.random in the client networking is the backoff jitter, which decorrelates racing human submits and never enters the action log or physics.
recommendation: No action required. Keep Math.random strictly out of shared/src/engine (it currently is). If a future refactor moves retry logic, ensure the jitter source stays out of any replayed path.

title: No fetch has a timeout/AbortController; a hung resyncLog in the fire watchdog traps the player in 'Sending…' permanently
evidence: recoverStuckFire(): `await this.resyncLog(); if (this._isFiring) this.failFire(...)`. resyncLog() does `await this.supabase.from('room_actions').select(...)` with NO AbortController/timeout (grep confirms zero AbortController/signal usage in client/src). The fire watchdog (setFiring) already cleared itself (this.fireWatchdog=null) BEFORE invoking recoverStuckFire, and recoverStuckFire does not re-arm a watchdog.
impact: If the resync fetch hangs (TCP black-hole, captive portal, server stall) rather than erroring, the await never resolves, the `if (this._isFiring) failFire()` line is never reached, and no second watchdog is armed — so _isFiring stays true forever and the player is stuck in the 'Sending…' lock with no recovery short of a page reload. The FIRE_TIMEOUT_MS (9s) watchdog was designed to prevent exactly this stuck-lock, but it can only fire once and then defers to an unbounded fetch.
recommendation: Wrap supabase queries and edge-function fetches in an AbortController with a timeout (e.g. 8-10s), and in recoverStuckFire treat a resync that does not complete within a bound the same as a failure (release the lock + notify). Alternatively re-arm a short fallback timer around the resync await so the lock is always eventually released.
acceptance_criteria: A hung network during fire-recovery still releases the input lock within a bounded time
Supabase reads/edge POSTs are bounded by an AbortController timeout

title: tickToCompletion 10k-tick cap logs and returns, leaving the engine wedged in FIRING/RESOLVING so the client silently freezes for the rest of the match
evidence: tickToCompletion(): `while ((phase==='FIRING'||phase==='RESOLVING') && ticks<MAX_TICKS) {...} if (ticks>=MAX_TICKS) console.error('hit tick cap — possible corrupt engine state')`. On hitting the cap it ONLY logs and returns; the engine is left in FIRING/RESOLVING. flushPendingActions only drains while phase is PLAYER_TURN/ROUND_OVER, so every subsequent buffered action accumulates and never applies — the board freezes with no user-visible error (only a console line). During initialize() replay the same cap leaves a late-joiner permanently stuck mid-flight while nextExpectedSeq has already been set to the full row count.
impact: A single engine state that fails to leave FIRING/RESOLVING within 10k ticks (e.g. a runaway fire field, a settle that oscillates, or a corrupt projectile) silently and permanently desyncs/freezes that client for the rest of the match with no recovery path and no UI signal. The cap prevents an infinite loop (good) but converts it into a silent permanent stall.
recommendation: On hitting the cap, force the engine into a recoverable state (e.g. clear projectiles+fire and set phase to PLAYER_TURN, or surface a fatal-desync banner offering reload/leave) rather than returning with the engine wedged. Emit a fireFailed/connection-style signal so the UI is not silently frozen.
acceptance_criteria: Hitting the tick cap produces a user-visible signal, not just a console.error
The engine is never left wedged in FIRING/RESOLVING such that all future actions are dropped

title: RESOLVING phase with a null pendingSettle relies on a single defensive resolve(); a non-converging settleStep would spin RAF forever
evidence: tick()'s RESOLVING branch: if pendingSettle!==null it calls settleStepAnimated() and only resolve()s when it returns false (converged). settleStep is documented to converge in <=ceil(H/pxPerTick) calls and is solid-count-conserving, so it terminates in practice. There is no independent watchdog/iteration cap on the RESOLVING phase itself — termination depends entirely on settleStep always eventually returning false.
impact: If settleStep ever failed to converge for a column range (it currently cannot, given the strictly-downward sand model — hence observed:false and lower confidence), the engine would stay in RESOLVING and the RAF loop would tick it forever, freezing the turn machine. tickToCompletion's 10k cap would bound the synchronous replay path but the live RAF path has no equivalent cap on RESOLVING.
recommendation: Add a defensive iteration cap on the RESOLVING settle (force resolve() after N ticks) mirroring the FIRING MAX_FLIGHT_TICKS guard, so a future settleStep change that breaks convergence degrades to an early resolve rather than an infinite freeze.
acceptance_criteria: The RESOLVING phase cannot tick unboundedly even if settleStep stops converging

title: flushPendingActions clears the firing lock on ANY drained action, so an interleaved opponent buy can release 'Sending…' one seq before the local fire echoes
evidence: flushPendingActions() calls `this.setFiring(false)` for every action it drains ('our action (or any) echoed → release the input lock + watchdog'). setFiring(false) also disarms the fire watchdog. If a turn-neutral buy (lower seq) is buffered and drained immediately before the local player's fire row (next seq), the lock+watchdog are cleared on the buy, before the fire has actually committed/echoed.
impact: Not a desync (the fire still applies in seq order when its row arrives, and the engine phase-gates correctly), but it defeats the watchdog's purpose for that window: if the local fire's submit was in fact lost, the lock was already released by the unrelated buy so the 'Shot timed out — try again' recovery never fires for that shot, and the HUD briefly shows the input as ready before the fire row lands (flicker). The release is keyed on 'any log advance' rather than 'my pending action committed'.
recommendation: Track the seq (or a token) of the locally-submitted turn-ending action and only clear the firing lock when THAT row is drained, not on any drained action. Keeps the watchdog meaningful per-submit.
acceptance_criteria: The firing lock is released only when the local player's own submitted action commits, not on an unrelated buffered action

title: VERIFIED CLEAN: shared/src engine has no wall-clock or unseeded randomness; the lockstep determinism invariant holds
evidence: grep for Date.now|performance.now|Math.random|new Date across shared/src returns only doc-comment mentions, zero actual calls. Physics.stepProjectile uses a fixed dt (no clock). Wind (nextWind) and terrain (generate) draw from independent seeded mulberry32 streams (Random.createRng / Terrain.hashSeed). AI aim error is seeded off (turn, tankId, wind) via createRng. clone() reconstructs the wind RNG by re-seeding windRngSeed and fast-forwarding windRngCalls, and deep-copies every mutable field — so a clone replays an identical future. effectiveGravity/currentGravity are pure functions of (turn, suddenDeathTurn). syncFire sorts by x for a stable snapshot; computeMatchWinner/advanceTurn iterate stable array order.
impact: Positive control: the repo's prime invariant (bit-identical engine across hot-seat and every networked client) is upheld in the engine code as written. The reliability risks to lockstep are therefore NOT in the engine's math but in the network/state-plumbing layer (cursor trust reliability-003, silent wedge reliability-006, lock release reliability-008).
recommendation: Maintain a CI guard (lint rule or the existing scripts/checks harness) that fails if Date.now/performance.now/Math.random ever appears under shared/src/engine, so the invariant cannot regress silently.
acceptance_criteria: A check fails the build if wall-clock or Math.random is introduced under shared/src/engine
